parentQuestion: A 45-year-old woman presents with hyperactive knee and ankle reflexes bilaterall...

scaffolds.q: Upper motor neuron lesions cause hyperactive reflexes BELOW the level of the lesion.
scaffolds.explain: Yes — and And now, the revelation:. Upper motor neurons normally provide INHIBITION to spinal reflexes. When you damage them, you RELEASE the lower motor neurons from inhibition — reflexes become exaggerated and brisk. This is why stroke patients develop hyperreflexia. Receive this insight:the irony: losing control creates too much response.

scaffolds.q: Lower motor neuron lesions cause ABSENT reflexes at the level of damage.
scaffolds.explain: Absolutely — and this is where the anatomy becomes devastation. Lower motor neurons ARE the efferent limb of the reflex arc. Destroy them, and the reflex cannot happen — you've cut the wire that makes the muscle contract. The reflex arc is BROKEN. this is the key to locating spinal lesions.
